text.title: Messaging House Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every messaging house must enforce. These prevent the most common failure: per-channel divergence where the buyer hears different value props in different places.

rule.statement: Exactly ONE root value statement at the top. One sentence, max 18 words, includes who-it's-for + what-it-does + outcome. Reject houses with 2+ root statements OR statements exceeding 18 words.
rule.rationale: Multiple root statements confuse buyers; long statements lose the spine. 18 words forces clarity.

rule.statement: Exactly THREE pillars supporting the root. Not 2 (too thin), not 4+ (loses focus). Each pillar names a discrete area of value. Reject houses with pillar count != 3.
rule.rationale: Buyers retain three; four becomes "a list." Three is the canonical positioning pillar count for a reason.

rule.statement: Each pillar carries 2-4 proof points. Each proof point is one of: data (with source), customer quote (with named attribution), demo asset (with link). Reject pillars with < 2 proof points OR proof points without evidence type.
rule.rationale: Pillars without proof are claims; claims without evidence don't convert. Each pillar needs at least two evidence types so a buyer with one objection still finds another to lean on.

rule.statement: Each pillar produces per-channel variants for ≥ 3 channels (X, LinkedIn, email, ads, landing). Variants must preserve the pillar's core idea AND respect the channel's character limits + voice. Reject variants that drift to a different pillar.
rule.rationale: Buyers exposed to consistent messaging across 3+ channels convert 2-3× higher. Variants that drift fragment the buyer's mental model.

rule.statement: The house has a named owner (content lead) who approves all per-channel variants before publish. Reject houses without a single named owner OR houses where individual channel writers commit variants directly.
rule.rationale: Distributed ownership leads to local optimization (each writer optimizes their channel; collective consistency suffers). One owner enforces consistency at the gate.

example.title: Filled messaging house
example.code:
Root value: "Faion gives solo developers shipped product in weeks, not months, by combining a Claude Code add-on with 1300+ methodologies."
Pillars: P1: 10× output. Same budget. Proof: customer quote (alex@indie), data: 3 launches in 90 days P2: One person, full SDD lifecycle. Proof: demo: 7-min spec → ship video, customer quote (jess@solo) P3: Methodology depth, not just templates. Proof: data: 52 skills × 1300 methodologies, customer quote (sam@dev)
Per-channel variants: X (P1): "Solo dev. 3 launches in 90 days. With Faion." LinkedIn (P1): [200 chars expanded with quote + outcome] Email subj (P2): "Spec → ship in one afternoon" Landing hero (root): the root statement, full sentence
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r3, r4 in a complete house.

example.title: Pillar inflation, weak proof points
example.code:
Root: "We help businesses transform with cutting-edge AI solutions." (24 words, vague)
Pillars: P1 Innovation, P2 Quality, P3 Speed, P4 Cost-effective, P5 Reliable, P6 Scalable Proof: each pillar has 1 generic claim, no evidence.
Per-channel: all variants say "transform with AI" — no pillar differentiation. Buyer hears the same vague thing in 5 places.
example.note: Illustrates r1, r2, r3, r4 simultaneous violations.
